There is a chance that one of us is going to die on this call and we're just going to have to push module five to next week. Hopefully that's not the case. I mean, I wanna make sure people don't get too wound up about theme and narrative arc and other things because if you make a capital T theme, then it feels like there's weight associated. To me, the theme is just home base. What the hell is this thing about? From beginning to end, if there's a thing that runs through all of it, what is that thing? So in Julie's example, if the overall theme is natural movement, and first of all, you need to make that clear to the audience, what do you mean by natural movement as opposed to unnatural movement? Like that's, to me, these are the kinds of questions that come from me out of theme and that can get, and then I would want to connect that to this sub-idea of lifestyle, right? It's probably not natural that we sit in chairs all day. So that's a good way to connect larger theme to sub-theme. So, okay, Julie, just posted, it's how you move when you're in nature. Okay, so sitting in an ergonomic chair is not how we move in nature, right? So Parkour and other things are based on this idea of natural movement. So it would be, first of all, I need to understand, does my audience know what I'm talking about? Or if I'm contrasting natural movement with sort of an unnatural movement, like they go into the gym and they do these sort of fixed movements in machines and why that wouldn't make a lot of sense. So it's the natural movement and the lifestyle things are woven together. Now, which is the larger theme and which is a sub-theme? I mean, to me, it would seem like in this example, natural movement is the larger theme. Like, and it's almost comparing and contrasting. It's because natural movement is a beautiful example of an implication of an open loop, right? At the moment you say natural movement, my first question is, well, what the hell is unnatural movement? There's implication there. So that immediately creates tension. So you're off to the races already with a good theme. And then it's a matter of making sure you don't reduce that tension too quickly. And where is it going? That's all, that also informs a lot of themes. You know, can you, if the theme is natural movement and you say everything you need to say in the first email about natural movement, then the theme isn't natural movement because the theme has to carry the freight from beginning to end of the series. I mean, that's, I don't know if that's how the word really, I don't feel like we're answering this question well, but Julie, feel free to drop more details into the question on the, in Zoom and we'll come back at this because I'm not sure if we may have needed to ask additional questions. Yeah, I think I'll just quickly add something that you mentioned earlier, that you mentioned when you started on from the question and I just want to draw attention to it because it's important. Whenever I write something and share it with my writing coach and then she comes back and she says, you needed to find what that means. And she's always picking at me because in my head, I know what I mean. And people won't necessarily, just because I think that most of our audience know what a pop means, a pocket of people. Well, that should be defined. So then someone reading it that maybe doesn't quite know what that means, then everybody's on the same Himmishid and then they can enjoy the impact of the series. So, yeah, so just more broadly, we need to define what we mean. So it's clear because when things aren't clear, then it's difficult for people's attention to stay focused. And that's the curse of knowledge right there. And all of us deal with this. It's not just Julie in this example. All of us have the curse of knowledge. When you know something about a topic, it's very easy to fill in the things that people who don't know about the topic don't know. And we just, we can fill in assuming they know those things and it's really have to take a step back and ask them to say, like, if, how would I explain this to someone who does not know anything about this? When we use a term or we anything, we have to take a step back and say, well, wait a minute, what does that mean? Because it creates incredible opportunities when we do that, because there's just so much more we can explore when we don't automatically fill in the blanks. All right, these next questions are from Rich. It looks like there are like 45 questions in this one group of questions. So I'm going to, I'm going to do these in two parts, I think. We both leverage narratives so much. Okay, so, all right, All right, Andre, do you want to tackle this first? Where do we start? How do we, because I think we probably have different answers to this. Maybe I don't know, we'll be curious to see if we have any coherent answers to this at all. I think, broadly speaking, maybe most people's entry point in will be a product. They'll have something, they would have codified some expertise and put it into this thing, whether it's free or paid. And that becomes the natural entry point. I think that's where lots of people start. Even if they build in audience first, they'll be known for something where they'll try and put themselves out there around an idea. Yeah, then I think building a personal brand where the brand, this idea, is just a way of thinking and seeing the world that's maybe slightly more challenging because it's not a specific thing, it's not a product. So, for us at TLB, we have a more general entry point, but then we have all of the courses there that create framings. So, these all these different things that people come into our world thinking about, or they're there for a certain reason. So, we send them through our more general thing and then through the context of that, we use automation to try and help connect them with points of interest and then take it from there. There's no right or wrong answer, it's just how you choose to do it. But I think it's easier to start with a product. I think that's my gut feeling anyway. I mean, that's our start, I think. And it's really, it comes down to a question. If you get on an airplane, how do you get from point A to point B? Well, the first thing you need to know is where's point B, if you get a plane like, hey, I'd like to go somewhere. Where, right? And that's the first question. So, do we, this is, I'll think out loud for a second. I think this is how I think about it. I say that because at this point, it's unconscious competence, I just do it and then who knows. So, yes, you have to know where you're going because the reason you have, from my perspective, the reason you need to know where you're going is because then there's no contrast without A and B. A is where they are, B is where you're taking. In order to get the, in order to have that contrast built in, that structural tension that Robert Fritz talks about so much, you have to have those two points. You have to have this desired future reality and then their current relationship to that future reality. And if there's no distance between those things, then there's no tension. So, yes, you have to start with that. And the way I think about it is, I start with where am I taking them and what are the emotions associated with that destination? When they get there, how are they going to feel? And then, where are they now? And how do they feel right now? And what's the delta between how they feel now and how they want to feel? And in that space, in the difference between how they currently feel and how they would like to feel is all of your playground. Because ultimately, it's just that old saying, we buy with emotion and we justify with logic. We are emotional creatures. We want to feel differently. If we didn't buy, we wouldn't buy things if we didn't want to feel differently. That's at the end of the day, that there's nothing more true than that. So I have to understand, to do this work well, I have to understand how somebody wants to feel, how I think they want to feel as a result of whatever it is I'm showing them. And it could just be information. It doesn't have to be, I mean, it could be an SOS without a product at the end. It could just be an idea that I want somebody to internalize. And then that once they understand this idea, then they're going to feel better as a result. And so it doesn't always have to be a product, but there does have to be a destination because a destination sets up a tension and it tells us what the emotions are. And then this question here, where do we start? What's the anchor to build from? I mean, you build from A to B tension, where they are, where they want to go, the emotional difference between those things. And then, you know, this is where it's just magic. Like sometimes there's a quote, sometimes there's an idea that pops into your head. Like when pride popped into our head in the conversation for the AOE enrollment, like something just shows up. And it's, and that may be different for me because I need a starting point. I can't just sit down and start writing and hope I wind up someplace. I need to know where I'm starting. It could be a scene from a movie, right? One of the emails we wrote a year and a half or so ago, was about an experience I had when I was in, working with a client in Baltimore. And it was a scene from a movie, from a movie Heat. Like that thing was, like that was the anchor that everything came from in that email because that was such a poignant experience. So that's, yeah, I mean, I think I'll leave it at that. I'll just move on to the next question. You good with that? Do you want anything to add before we move on to the next question? No, no, no, go for it. I'm just gonna have like give myself time to think about stuff. Yeah, I like the way that Rich has framed that. Yeah, I don't have any issues with that. I don't think. Do we see our customers as our heroes, as the main character, the hero? Yeah, I think partly what we do, some of the people that we serve are people that serve others. So we serve creators that have their own audiences. So partially one of the things we do is we try and make them look smart in front of their customers by helping them see the world in a different way. And you can go backwards as well. At some point, we've all had our own journeys and had to battle along the way. And through that journey, we've learned things. If you wanna follow the hero's journey, at some point, we become a guide to somebody else. And through that lens, we can then help them move forward. And I think in that context, they are the hero. We helping them on their journey through our own expertise and the fact that we've been on that journey. And yeah, we can give them tools, products, ideas, insights that I've said this so many times where those things can metaphorically be magical items. And it's through those magical items that are not gonna get them necessarily directly to their destination, to point B, but it's gonna help them get there. There's always gonna be obstacles along the way. I feel like I'm rambling off the path now. So bring us back. I mean, this is straight out of Donald Miller's story brand playbook, right? So the customer is the hero. The hero has an external emotion that is indicative of a deep internal need. And then the hero meets the guide. The guide has empathy first, authority next, and the guide has a plan. And the plan is related to a future desired reality which is contrasted with a future that they don't want. Like that story branded a nutshell. The challenge I've always had with Donald Miller's framework is that it doesn't tell you how to create a campaign. It tells you how to think about your audience in relationship to a campaign. But then if you don't sit down and think to yourself, like, okay, let me name our hero. And I'm going to tell the story about the hero who meets me and I'm the guide. It just, it gets really weird. So it's, I think it's a thinking tool, right? So the first thing you need to imagine, and again, this maps to what we talked about a second ago, there's contrast, right? The hero feels a certain way. Your customers feel a certain way right now. There's an external manifestation of that, which could be like frustration. Like they're trying to get something done and they don't know how to do it. But that what really matters is the internal sense they have as a result. Maybe they feel like a failure because they're of this frustration. And when you, you can speak to the frustration and scratch the surface, or you can speak to the sense of failure and really light the other person up. Because sometimes your audience hasn't made that connection. Like they have a soup of feelings and they all get jammed together and they don't feel good as a result. They want to feel better. And the first thing we do is we sort of deconstruct for them that there's, what are those feelings? Why are they important? And be very clear that they're valid, right? I mean, we've all experienced this. We've all felt like, I assume, I know I certainly have, we've all felt like failures at one time or another. We've all felt frustrated at one time or another. But when we speak to these core deep visceral needs, or at least when we recognize them, that's the source of empathy. That's how we connect with an audience and say, I get it, I've been there, I've felt what you feel, and I know what it feels like to feel better and I can show you how. Like that's it. The rest, everything else just details. If you can do those two things really well, if you can define for somebody of future where they feel different and explain that to them, and you can compare that to where they are, and then you can honestly say that I can take you from point A to point B. Everything else just happens. Everything else are sort of details. I'm gonna tackle this one first. So Gustav, I have a 15 kilowatt or 13 kilowatt, $46,000 solar array city in my backyard that was installed this summer. So let me tell you about my user behavior. I reached out to them. I did exactly what you're talking about. I reached out before I bought two years later, right? I initially was going to do the installation. I, for some variety of reasons, two or three years, didn't do the installation. I went back to the company that had maintained the relationship and had connected me with the information I needed to make a good decision. And I didn't get a quote from a single one of their competitors. Didn't price shop, I never looked around after that. So my first recommendation to you is do not assume that user behavior is as cut and dry as you've described it. People make decisions in absolutely non-linear ways. And I get it. Lead gen feels, by definition, it's linear, right? And it's like, okay, someone's, you know, it's easy to look and say, okay, someone's, you know, signing up to essentially a lead aggregator and those leads, it's, you know, first come, first served, whoever picks up the phone first gets the, that's, that is a segment of the audience, yes. But I suspect a much larger segment of the audience. If for no other reason than the price point, right? This is, you know, this is, solar is not something in general where someone has that money lying around on the top of their dresser. And they're like, okay, well, I'm gonna go, you know, let's see, I got three things on my to-do list today. You know, brush teeth, wash car, buy solar array. Like, that's not how this works. Like this is an in-depth decision. So you can have a competitive advantage simply by teaching people how to think about hiring the right installer. Like, what do you know that they don't? What questions should they ask first? How can you position yourself as the trusted guide who is not rushing them? Because everybody else in your industry is rushing, rushing, rushing, because they all have the same belief that you do, which is that it's, you know, who gets there first gets the sale. So everybody's subject to that. If you want to feel, if you want to give the impression of being just like everybody else in your industry, then do it the way everybody else does. Or the very first thing that you could do is say, listen, I understand that a purchase of this size is significant. So the first thing we're going to do is we're going to, you know, go through whatever. Like it's, it's being that voice that's not, either voice that doesn't sound like everybody else, either one that they want to actually look and say, who's this because this seems different. It feels like a rant. Any thoughts on this? My only thought is one that, you know, we can only control what we can control. And thinking about or stressing over the things that we have no control over doesn't seem to be a valuable strategy. So, you know, if you're in a market where people shop around, well, then you're in a market where people shop around. But as Sean said, there's going to be enough people that value relationships. And I don't know how competitive that is, but I would have thought that all things being equal, you know, building and fostering good relationships can't be a bad thing. Second part of this question is regarding the idea of serialized narrative, should it start at the, abbreviate it, SLI, should it start at the sphere of influence or should it begin in the email series? So, for everyone who's not familiar with sphere of influence, you know, the sphere of influence is built on this idea that there's a, in general, a multi-page entry point prior to opt-in, where we're not offering a bribe, I mean, as a quote unquote ethical bribe to get somebody to opt-in that we're taking a position, opening and closing loops and drawing attention forward so that the opt-in is part of a larger journey for the right prospects. It's an easy no for people who are not pulled forward and an easy yes for people who want to learn more without the addition of some kind of bribe to get the email address because when we do that, we get a much muddier signal than if it's through that pure, just intellectual interest or emotional desire. So, how do you want to think about this question, Andres? I mean, serialized narratives and their relationship to sphere, I just kind of answered it when I described it. Yeah, I mean, the sphere of influence inspired front-end always is always going to come first. That's where people have to get through before they can end up on an email list. So that context matters. It helps shape the quality and the frame that somebody has when they come through essentially your front door, which is your opt-in, which is why, definitely, I'm not a fan of having these really short, blind opt-ins and make it really easy for somebody to get their free thing because then you don't really have the attention other than you've delivered the thing that they've wanted or that they've come on your form once they've got that, their little free guard, then they're off and maybe you see them again and then maybe you don't. So, you can have the world's best relationship with the old series narrative arc on there. And if somebody's just there to get something and then they're off in the sense that the attention is elsewhere, there's nothing you can do to save that really. So, yeah, it all starts before somebody even gets onto your email list, but that's a conversation for a completely different product. And this will be our first reference to Squared and Dopamine today, right? So, one of the problems with the lead magnet is it's an artificial Squared of Dopamine that makes you feel better and you don't have to do anything. You don't have to consume it. You can go and you can read an opt-in page like others. You're gonna get this XYZ video series, this and that. It's gonna change your world and on and on and on. Great, and all I need to do is give you my email address. Cool, I'll give you my email address. Boom, we got access to it, Squared of Dopamine. I don't need to do anything now. There's no more attention. I've got the thing, I can go check it out whatever I want to. Right, it's just, it's so counterproductive as opposed to generating excitement and then opt-in gets them to the next thing that they need to continue pulling their attention for that's opening even additional loops. Like you're continuing, you're not artificially closing attention or ending the attention that a lead magnet does. And people who download lead magnets tend to be in Facebook will certainly can confirm this. I mean, if you optimize for leads, you get lots of people who become leads and they're not necessarily people who become customers. So anyway, we'll go into that sideways. This next one, Elvaro. All right, module three soap opera series, this is Francis, can you share your thoughts on using multiple themes in a single soap opera series? Like mixing good versus evil, us versus them, feels like they could go well together. But also I'm not sure if my understanding of themes is strong enough that I'm missing a glaring issue. Why don't you tackle this one first? Yeah, I think we almost covered this on the very first question. I'm gonna stick to my own sense, I think we should say keep it simple and just have a single controlling idea, this idea of a thesis or a theme. Just keep it as one thing. My coach shared with me very early when we started our relationship and she shared this thing with me, which is a structural wheel. And in the center of the structural wheel is this thing called thesis, your thesis statement, your controlling idea and everything, every single inside, every single thing that you wanna share that loops around this wheel almost like the spokes of a wheel. And then when it gets back to the top, then you would have finished the piece or the series or whatever it is. But it's the single idea that holds the whole thing together. I think it can get confusing when you're trying to layer on too many of these things together or at least thinking of it that way. I think it's fine to have these little sub ideas, but ultimately one series or one essay or one email needs to have a single controlling idea. So this idea of a theme, just keep it as a single thing. And if other things show up as you writing this thing, well, then that's fine, but ultimately they're all attached to this one arc, this one thing that joins everything together. Yeah, I think my, I don't know, I'll disagree with you just for the sake of disagreeing, because we never disagree. It's not even disagreeing, but you know, in this example, good versus evil, us versus them, that maps perfectly, so sure. But it's not too theme. Okay, now I'm gonna agree with you again, damn it. It's not two themes, it's one theme. I'm good, they're bad, right? That's the theme, you know, and sure as it mixes good versus evil, but you have to be able to say it in a sentence, right? Like what's this whole thing about? It's about A and B, right? What's the art of email manifesto about? That conventional wisdom is wrong, and if you follow it, you're going to get a suboptimal result, right? That's not the copywriting version of it. That's what we're saying, right? Now, is there a lot of nuance in that? Sure, you know, it's wrong, and because it's wrong, you do things that make sense, but they're all downstream consequences of a bad assumption. So, you know, there's layer upon layer, but it all returns back to, you know, conventional wisdom, bad, the way we do it, good, right? Us versus them, good versus evil. I mean, that's, it's there, but we never sat down and wrote good versus evil, us versus them, and we never crafted it that way. We just crafted it around an idea. And that's how to think about theme. It's just an idea, right? That's all, I mean, it's the thing that hangs the whole thing together. It's the thing that the whole thing is about, right? Like if someone says, what is this about? Whatever you say next, that's the theme, right? That's it. That's the beginning and the end. Okay, next question. This is Mark. I can't wait to answer this question. I'm going to let you answer this question first, because I'm going to sound like a jackass when I answer this question, you know, we'll do good cop, bad cop. All right, here's Mark's question. If we're opening and closing lots of loops across the sequence, how do we deal with people who don't read every email, which is most people? Do we just not worry about them because they're not our most engaged people? Who's about having links to earlier emails so they can read ones they've missed, or is there something else we can deliberately do that people who dip into just one email still get the value? Okay, so question. If we're opening and closing lots of loops across the sequence, how do we deal with people who don't read every email? Andre, good cop. I'm going to be the good cop. Well, I mean, my first thing I'm going to say is that we can't stop, you know, we can't force people's attention. Yet we can't make people pay attention. We can only do our thing. And some people are going to pay attention, and some people aren't. The whole idea of, you know, building and making an email list that is very targeted, and there's a strong relationship, means that by default, people are going to be paying attention more often than not. But at the same time, attention is different to... I'm just going to go on a slight tangent here quickly. The way that I see it is that attention and trust are two different things. So trust is something that you have. It's something that you build, and you have a certain level of trust, and over time that level of trust increases, just like a relationship with a friend who starts off as a stranger. And just because you don't see that person every single day, there can be a whole year that goes by, and you haven't seen the person. But when you see the person, it's like best friends again. Like you've never... Like you've just, you know... Like the last time that you saw them was yesterday, not a year ago. So attention is kind of like an oscilloscope that goes up and down because people have lives, and their attention will phase in and phase out. This is one of the reasons why we do the whole bridge email thing, is because it's a mechanism to pull someone's attention forward for a certain journey. And those people that just don't have the attention at that point, even though you have a strong relationship with them, now when they face with this thing, maybe now it isn't the right time, their attention is elsewhere. Something's happened, they've lost a job or whatever. So those people typically won't respond, and therefore you don't have the attention. So by creating these mechanisms that focus people's attention, you're more likely to have their attention. And then so now that you know that you're more likely, you've done something that's increased your chances that you're gonna have the attention of most people in this pool. Then you use your open loops. I wouldn't throw a kitchen sink of open loops in a single email because I think that can be counterproductive. But an open loop, like a single powerful open loop I think is more powerful than like a whole bunch of them because then it just gets messy, your mind can't. But if this is one thing like the previous question that we, who was it from? But the module two, where we mentioned at the end that the insight is gonna be obvious when you see it. That's a single open loop that we put at the end and it's driving that person really in it. I'm sure it's driving many people insane, but it's not a small board of open loops. It's just that one strategically placed at the end that's gonna drive enough people insane that they're gonna reread that lesson, I don't know how many times. So I'll stop there for now. And I'm quite curious to see what the backup component is gonna be. I'm just trying to get over the fact that you said that awareness is like an oscilloscope. What the hell are you, what? And Google oscilloscope on when you were talking to figure out what you meant. So I'm not trying to be bad cop to you, but that is kind of funny. All right, so here's the question. If we're opening and closing lots of loops across the sequence, how do we deal with people who don't read every email? My answer is write better emails. I mean, it sounds like the most obvious answer in the world. And I'm not trying to be a jackass, but that's the deal, right? You gotta figure out the thing that grabs people by the throat and grabs their attention that actually matters. If you're not talking about the things that actually matter, people will ignore it. We all would. I mean, there's no open loop in the world that's gonna pull somebody's attention towards something they don't give a shit about. It's just that's not possible. So the thing you've gotta figure out is what keeps this person up at night? Like, what's the thing that if they could solve right now would meaningfully change their life, like significantly change their life? Because if you're talking about that, then you're going to have their attention from beginning to end. And if you're talking about that and they're not paying attention, then they're never going to pay attention, right? They clicked on it for who knows why, but the people who are getting your emails, if you're speaking to the thing that keeps them awake at night that matters more to them than just about anything else, that if they could just solve it, their life would be infinitely better. Finally, you talk about that, you don't have to worry about anything else. So that's the longer answer. All right, next question. I mean, that's one of the reasons why we've made the audience not a masterclass free for any customer of ours, because it's so important. All that stuff that you learn, if you actually did that work, would inform lots and lots of downstream ideas. Yeah, so that's what I'm saying. And it's shocking. I mean, it's shocking how, why people buy things. It's never why we think people buy things, ever. It's, there's so much nuance to it, but it's there's, when you peel back those layers of nuance and you speak to that thing that's really driving the action, then you'll have the right people's attention. You won't have everybody's attention because everybody's not paying attention in any moment of time. Even when you raise their hand, right? All right, let's go on to the next one. So many questions. Okay, you say email frequency can be adjusted to match your needs and the needs of your audience. Do you have any suggestions for determining an audience's preference for email frequency? When I've asked directly in the past, or when I've reviewed unsolicited comments that people send me, I end up with a huge range of conflicting responses. Okay, Andre, anything on email? I think the first answer with that is, is to be careful what you ask for. Yeah, never ask a question. You don't want an answer to. That's rule number one. Yeah, I think a different way of asking a question like that would be, do you want the weekly summary or do you want the whole thing? You know, something like that. So it's a this or a that. And then you're acknowledging that certain people have different levels of attention and a once a week, so the once a week summary would be every Saturday. They click yes for that because they're busy and that sounds about right. But when it's too, like, do you want it daily or every third day or then it just gets a bit out of hand and you're gonna get the whole spectrum of people having their opinions. I think that's the quick answer. Yeah, and I think, you know, the way I would tackle this is what do you mean when you ask for email frequency? What emails, right? Is it a newsletter? Well, I mean, every week, every two weeks, every month, whatever the hell you feel like it, that makes sense, right? I mean, depending on what you're giving to your audience, but if it's a mini course where someone needs to understand something and they need to sit down every day for 15 minutes and if they do that over the course of five days, that time is gonna give them a worthwhile result, then it needs to be daily. And you can let your audience know too that, listen, I've chosen, I wouldn't use this language, but basically say I've chosen the frequency for this series the way I have because it's the, from my perspective, knowing what I know, it's the best way for you to understand and internalize this information, right? I could tell this to you over the course of a month, once a week, but that would not have the same impact if I do this, if you sit down for 15 minutes a day over the next four days, like that's when you'll get the most and tell your audience that. Don't assume that they know why they're getting things at the frequency and those frequencies, they undulate, which would probably show up on an oscilloscope, I think. Isn't that true? Is an undulation on an oscilloscope, Andre? So what I mean by that is that you're not a metronome of communication, every 72 hours you get a thing from me. It can be more intense for a short period of time, longer durations for other periods of time. I mean, there's an ebb and a flow to everybody's attention and it really depends on what value you're creating for your audience and how that value needs to be delivered and consumed by them and you can tell them that on the front end. So there is no, there's no canonical right answer here. It is much more a sense of like, what's the best way for somebody to understand this, this information that I'm sharing with them to get the insights that I want them to have? Can they do that if they get this over four weeks? Or do they, is it better if they do that all at once? And if it is over longer periods of time, then it probably makes sense to refer back to the previous one. So you can always orient people in space or you can time, where you can also do that in the beginning. You see us do this a lot in beginnings of emails as we'll talk about like this summer when we did the four part courage and perseverance series, we would start each of those parts, number two, three and four by orienting to what sort of where we were. So lots of ways to do this. Okay, next question. How would you approach things if you suspect your audience prefers shorter emails? I just wanna say like find a new audience, but I'm not gonna be that guy. Split things out into emails. So each individual email is shorter. Leave longer gaps between emails. They have time to digest, make the emails as long as they need to be, get across your full message and assume that the right people read all the way through. You others don't, something else. So Andre, how would you approach things if you suspect your audience prefers shorter emails? You know, I think again, there's no, I mean, for us is there's a reason why we're not on Twitter, for example, I'm gonna go on a small range. There's a reason why we're not on Twitter is because people that enjoy snack-worthy content hang out on Twitter, for example. People that perhaps given the choice would rather read a 200 word email as opposed to a 1500 word or a thousand word email. We prefer for our audience, we prefer them to enjoy or demand deeper thoughts, which you can only unpack in a longer piece. So I think this is us and this is how we show up and this is part of our relationship building series. This is how we write our emails. This is how we show up. If this is too much for you, if it's too much information too deep, then unsubscribe and this is it. So I think establishing those ground rules early on. If you have an audience that likes short things and you give them short things, then everyone's gonna be happy. But if you're unhappy with the results you're getting from an audience, then perhaps you need to change it up and then set those ground rules out early on. So then people know what to expect. And that's it. Yeah, I think my first response to this is now 180 degrees different. Which is, my first response is, find a different audience, right? Like that's the lib and I think that's just me being an ornery dumbass at nine o'clock in the morning. And if your audience prefers short, I mean, first of all, how do you know is the first question? Like don't assume that your audience, then don't ask them, hey, would you like a shorter email? Because people don't know what they want. I mean, you have no idea what we want. I went, you know, we'll say like, oh, I prefer shorter emails, but then, you know, it's the same person who reads 1500 words beginning to end at the edge of his or her seat. So we don't generally know. So don't ask your audience if they prefer shorter emails, observe their behavior and see what they seem to respond to. And if you're going to write a short email, write a really good short email, right? One open loop, one, you know, one clear call to action, one something, give your audience something to do. Right? Don't just hand them a, here's, you know, here's the thought for today, go forth and conquer. Right? We've, the world is littered with garbage emails like that. Like to have something that's interesting that captures their attention as a new way to see the world and leave them with an action step to take. Like, you know, do this thing today or do this thing as a result or ask them a question or something. I mean, you don't have to write 1500 words, but there probably is a minimum at which you need, you need enough structure there to make it worthwhile. Or the email, you know, the short email you prefer to read becomes the short email that you never read because there's no value in it. Right? And you get to, you get short, short and value tend to go hand in hand in some weird curve where too short, not enough value, too long, too hard to extract the value. Somewhere in there, there's a sweet spot for you and your audience. It doesn't have to be about 1500 words, could be six or 700 words, but it needs to be enough that you're bringing their interest back for more. And it's really easy to think that you can short and short and short and you're doing somebody a favor by doing that. But I don't think that's true. Focus on the value and then value dictates how long the email should be. All right. Okay, these are three questions here. Let's see, I'll do these separately, I guess. Do you ever think of soap opera series mapping onto the stages of awareness or any other high level concept like that? Andre, have you ever, in the last 27 years, thought about mapping a soap opera series to stages of awareness? I'm a simple person. That's way beyond my pay grade. We could, right? I mean, sure. And for everybody who's not familiar with the stages of awareness, five stages of awareness from completely unaware, like someone has no idea that there's even a problem or that there are solutions to the problem. The other's problem awareness, there's solution awareness and it gets increasingly sophisticated. And you can imagine it left or right. And the less aware an audience is, the harder they are to convert, which is not rocket science, if you think about it. There's a sweet spot in there somewhere, but it's really who are you talking to? And how are you generating traffic? And from my perspective, that trying to go after completely unaware audiences, that makes no sense. It's dangerous. Yeah, it's a good way. Just find people who are already nudists and tell them about your nudist colony, right? Like, to me, that's all the wisdom you need in marketing, in one sentence. It just comes down to like, what does somebody need to internalize? What do they need to accept, believe, internalize to where the value that you create for them is the logical conclusion of that worldview? Like that's it. Okay. Yeah, that's a question. I think mini courses is a high order idea that I like to think about. And then within that, you know, how you want to present your point of view that's the first person piece, or, you know, it can be, and it doesn't have to be third person. You can break into third person if you sharing a case study. And case studies can be injected within these containers. It's not necessarily its own thing. So, you know, the point of view and case studies can be used inside of things like mini courses or your narrative. Jonathan Baker, who we've, I think we had a series last year where we drew attention to him. I think we interviewed him too. I can't remember. Yes, we did. But to me, Jonathan Baker was like, he's a perfect example of how this idea works. And Jonathan Baker works with elite cyclists and to go faster on a bicycle, essentially. And the framing for, I think he has a mini course, but the framing is very clear. And it's so compelling. The framing, you know, he has so much expertise in this field. And he bases that there are three ways to go faster on a bicycle. You can improve the aerodynamics, reduce the weight or generate more power. Reducing, you know, improving the aerodynamics and shaving off weight are very expensive, low impact solutions. If you want to go faster on a bicycle, you need to generate more power. And that's what Jonathan teaches cyclists. He creates custom training plans for people to generate more power to go faster on a bicycle. I mean, that to me, it's such a perfect example of framing, right? It's an expert saying to you, if you're trying to do X, here are the three ways that you can do X. Two of them don't make any sense once I explain them to you. You spend a ton of money for a wind tunnel and other things. And it's not going to really help. You can spend thousands of dollars for wheels and shave off, you know, grams of weight, but you're not really going any faster. If you want to go faster, you have to generate more power. And that's what he spent a career, working with cyclists to generate more power on the bicycle. It's so brilliant because by the time you're exposed to his messaging, you reach the inescapable conclusion that if you want to go faster on a bicycle, first of all, A, you need to generate more power, and B, Jonathan has spent a career training people how to do that. So you're clearly in the right place. That's a simple version, but that's an empire building version of this idea. So all right, next question. Yeah, and then, you know, Jonathan's gonna talk in his first person point of view, but then he can also break into customer case studies where he's, you know, let me tell you about Frank, and then he'll break into third person within that narrative. So you can mix and match these things. And by doing that, it creates this very compelling narrative that's going to maintain someone's attention all the way through. That model works really well just for everybody to think about. That model works exceptionally well across, I think, any market. If you can orient to somebody, I mean, think about the benefit of this whole structure. Number one, this is to be able to say to somebody, this is what you want, right? This is what you're trying to accomplish. And then to say, and these are the X number of ways that you can get from where you are to there. And here are all the ways that don't make any sense for these reasons. And this is what does make sense. Like that is such, that's what experts do. I think I've called this in the past like the expert frame. And you don't have to be capital E expert. You just have to know what you're talking about enough to say all of those things. And in doing that, it's such a gift for the person who's experiencing it to feel like, okay, someone's sort of explained this whole thing to me. I get it now. Like this is the high level view that they've indicated where I'm trying to go. And I'm like, yup, that's where I'm trying to go. They've shown me the ways I could get there. I've heard about a lot of these things in the past. And they've also shown me like where each one makes sense or where they don't. And now, and yes, of course, the thing that you're recommending is the thing that you do. But you've made a really compelling case for it. And it makes sense. And that's very, very, very powerful. Especially when you're inside a topic that somebody doesn't know something about. And you can show them what it looks like inside the tent. Okay, Alvaro asked the question. There's a lot of background with this, but I don't think we need the background. So the question is this, how can we avoid a situation where a person goes through different soap opera series simultaneously? I assume there's a technical answer to this question. Yeah, so, well, it'll almost be impossible from the sense that soap opera series is something that gets triggered within the context of something else, of a sequence that someone's going through or through a newsletter, there's a link inside there. I mean, they can click on that single link multiple times. It's not gonna restart their progress inside of it, but there's no other soap opera series for them to click on. Let me read the rest of it then to give you some context. So, is this, I can understand where this could go wrong. So let's say for the sake of argument, I'll summarize what he said. Let's say for the sake of argument that someone gets to the end of our relationship building series and we have to choose your own adventure email. Or you can say, hey, if you're interested in generating more awareness for your business, XYZ and engagement and conversion, and someone says, oh, hell yeah, I wanna know about awareness. And they click on something that triggers the soap opera series for the traffic engine. But then they're looking at their email and they're like, oh, hell, not only that, I wanna understand conversion too, because those two things are super important. Then they click on the link for the soap opera series for... All right, okay. So how do we manage, like, you know, and I'm gonna assume this is just a tag-based thing, like something starts, something ends, you can't do two things simultaneously. Yeah, I mean, there's multiple ways to approach this. And, you know, there's maybe a better way, maybe not. But this really depends on how you choose to do this. And obviously communicate what's gonna happen inside the body of the email that's framing this to your own adventure. So at the end of a relationship building series, for example, if you decide to do a to your own adventure and there's three options, I would probably use a custom field. So it can only have a single value. And based on that, it'll only send them one thing. So you can communicate that, you know, choose one thing here. You can't choose multiple, because it'll just select the last thing that you've chosen. So you can say, just pick one of these three, you can later, there'll be an opportunity to go through another one. So that's one way to do it. You could also use tags and have a tag trigger one of these things. But then again, just communicate that if you're interested in all three of these things, you're gonna be getting three emails every single day for a week. So just, you know, so I think that's one way to do it. We're not gonna be prescriptive and tell you which way you should choose. That's just, you know, up to how you decide that you want somebody to interact with you. If multiple emails every single day is gonna be overwhelming, then just rig it in such a way that they can only choose one thing. I don't have pretty much to add to this other than, you know, I wouldn't overthink it. I mean, I think of my own buying behavior. If I click on three things, you send me three emails every day. I'm okay with that. And I understand the logic of one thing at a time. But when someone has indicated, I think a baseline principle that I live by is that people vote with their behavior. Right, so when someone stands up and says, you know, send me the info about conversion, and maybe they don't know what they're about to get into. But I think if someone says, hey, tell me about all of this stuff, like they just entered our world and they're gung-ho. I wanna know about it all. Click on it all, send it all, hold my beer, watch this shit. Like that's my, I don't know, that's kind of a bad answer. But I don't think there's a right answer here. Okay. Module four, relationship building series. Francis asked the question, do you recommend one relationship building series that everyone sees when they start, or there shouldn't be one for each core offer? We've got, I think we have about 275 variations of this question, so this is an important one. He said, you know, Francis says, we have several courses, each can stand alone to be enough similar to your setup. They're also fairly different, kind of complimentary. Someone purchasing one course can easily pick up another and add that service to their business. They could also only be interested in one specific service with zero interest in any others. I'll give Andrey a second to think about this one, and I'll just do a quick answer. So first of all, simple first, like, you know, the way there's a saying, I think it's from the theory of constraints. We're gonna march our elephants down a hallway, do it one at a time, right? So don't try to write four relationship building series simultaneously, because then you're not gonna create any value until every single one of them is complete, right? Write one relationship building series. Figure out whichever one's most valuable to you. Now, do you recommend one for everyone or for each core offer? That really depends on several factors. How are you generating traffic? If you're generating traffic at the product level, then you should have a relationship building series related to that product. If people are raising their hand to indicate interest at the product level, then yes, you need a relationship building series at the product level. If instead, your people are aware of you as like a group of experts, and they wanna have kind of a sense of, you know, how to make a decision about the products, then you need to educate them before they even know what they're looking for, then yes, you have one relationship building series that kind of gives them an overview. That's what we do. You know, we kind of say this is sort of what we believe and you know, the peers are core principles. This is why we think they're important. And at the end of the discussion of our core principles, that's the point at which we begin to talk about the fact that we do have some courses, but that's after we have, let you know where we stand on some things because courses don't make sense out of that context. However, if we decided to generate traffic at the product level, then we would have a relationship building series at the product level. And we would say, and we just talked about this recently, I think it was something recorded, but that yes, it was in the workshop, week two content. This is one of the things we discussed that you can have an entry point into your world that is a relationship building series that focuses more on the value the product offers versus the people behind the product. Any thoughts to add to that one, Andre? Yeah, I think, you know, all things being equal, you would probably want to have a relationship building series attached to every single product's front door and also a more general one. The way that we get around it, because as Sean said, you know, it requires a lot of bandwidth to be able to write multiple of these things. If you've got far products, then that's a lot to write. So one way to get around that is, so I'll ping a link into, I've targeted everybody, so it doesn't just go to myself and Sean. So that's the link to the Durable Business product page. Some of you have seen it and some of you haven't. And you'll notice that when we did a PLS last year or last year, I think it was, all of those emails are directly accessible on that page. So that's allowed us not to have to have a relationship building series opt-in on that page. We can, someone can land on that page and they can browse around, they can see all our old emails or from that single page. So they can have a similar experience without having to opt-in and then they'll come in through our general list because all roads lead to that on our site currently. So that's maybe an intermediary way of doing it. But ultimately, I think we would want to have a RBS on every doorway in, regardless of the stuff that we have on the product page. All of these assets that we put on our pages expand our world. So our building efforts attract people in their rank in the search engines, people come in, they land up on a page that's perhaps a sub-page of the product page and they browse around and they read this thing and it sucks them in and then they'll, at some point they'll be ready to opt-in. Maybe it's for their product, maybe it's they've now just been exposed to a whole world of stuff that they didn't even, that wasn't even in their awareness. And now they're on this amazing journey. So in that respect, world-building is very non-linear. And I know that that wasn't your question directly, but this, all of this is, comes from world-building. So we have the world-building on our website. And then when somebody's opting in, they're raising their hand to say that they want a bit more of this thing. And that's just a deeper level. And then the world-building comes online, comes into someone's inbox. So you can do it in these different layers. Yeah. I'll move on to the next question. So as Wolf says, what happens if someone unsubscribes during the relationship building series, do you still subscribe? These letters are absolutely not unsubscribed. They've unsubscribed. Now I suppose you could have a link at the top that says, hey, you're getting this relationship building series and you can unsubscribe from it, or there's a full nuclear unsubscribe in the footer. But we don't do that. And I don't, it's not, if someone doesn't go through the relationship building series, that's if they unsubscribe at that point, they're gone. And that's fine. And I mean, we're not going to make everybody happy. If someone tells you again, people vote with their behavior, if you've said, this is what you need to understand to be a good citizen in my world, to care about what I care about, to see the value in what I'm creating. And it's five emails, six emails, and I'm gonna walk you through it. And someone's like, ask through this, I don't like this. Why would you want them to stay? Like thank them. And there's no, it's not like, no one's bad, no one's good. It's just they're just not your people and they let you know. And thanks. Next question, if someone resubscribes down the road, but you send the RBS again, I say yes, Andre, what are your thoughts on these two questions? Yeah. So I'm just trying to think of what happens. If somebody unsubscribes, they're still on your list, but they get made inactive. I don't think they can resubscribe again at that point because they've done the full nuclear bomb unsubscribed. So although they exist as a lead inside of Conrad Kitt, for example, they're kind of grayed out and we have to manually reach out to the folks at Conrad Kitt and ask them to reactivate that account. We can delete that lead and then ask them to resubscribe and then they'll come in as a fresh new person again with the same email address. But it's normally an extra step for us, which we do because sometimes someone will reach out and say, hey, I think I unsubscribed at some point and now I'm trying to resubscribe again, but I'm not getting your emails. And that's the reason why. I presume or I'm just assuming that maybe not all ESPs choose to operate in the same way, maybe they do. Maybe there's some law saying that if somebody unsubscribes, then there needs to be a proper, valid reason for them to resubscribe again. All right, I'll move on to the next question also. From Carmen, there's a little background on this. It's a really interesting question though for any service providers out there. So the question is, how would you go about building a relationship building series and a soap opera series for a client when you're not an expert in that field? In the background info, he has a client that's a master at Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu. He does their marketing and advertising, and Carmen mentioned that he knows Jiu-Jitsu pretty well, but he's not an expert. So how do you write an AOE-inspired email series if you're not an expert? So I'll jump on this one, just because I'm familiar with Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu and why some of the ways to describe it to get people to be interested in it. So the first question you have to ask yourself is who's the audience that you're writing for and what do they care about? Because most likely, I mean, if this is an instructor who is trying to generate students locally, they don't care about anything at the level of mastery. They're not really, most of the people who are coming are going to be parents that were bringing their kids. So you're speaking to sort of a higher level view of what martial arts in general do for kids as far as self-esteem and other things. There's plenty of data out there about that, anecdotal and otherwise, easy to get parents to say, you know, little Joey came in and he was timid in this and that, and since he's been working with Master So-and-So, he's a totally different kid. I mean, martial arts transform kids. I know that I was 12 when I started Take One, though I've seen it happen hundreds, not thousands of times in my lifetime. I know it's true that martial arts completely transform kids and parents care about that. So you have to know the audience. Now, if this person is selling like Jiu-Jitsu instruction online to high level practitioners, it's a very different audience with a very different set of needs. And then it's not so much about the, it's about positioning this person, and how he or she, I assume, or how he or she can be valuable to that audience. That's a different, that's an entirely different approach. You don't have to be an expert in that, but you do need to understand, you do, so once you understand who the audience is, you need to understand what matters to that audience and most likely you're going to need to get some of that information from the client. So if it's the first audience and they're kids, if you're primarily parents bringing kids, then you just need to ask the question, ask this instructor, what do you think the benefits are to kids? Now, he or she may not know. I mean, I've been around plenty of martial artists in my lifetime and they don't always know. They just, they did the thing and now they teach the thing and whatever, some of them know. The danger is getting it wrong. One of the most compelling arguments for Jiu-Jitsu, you hear this over and over and over again, is the numbers change, but it's usually some variation of 85% of fights go to the ground and Jiu-Jitsu is a martial art that emphasizes ground fighting. So therefore, Jiu-Jitsu is the best thing. Everyone's going to go to the ground anyway. Might as well know something, be comfortable fighting on the ground. But that's not relevant to the mom or dad thinking about bringing their kid to train in Jiu-Jitsu. It can be completely true. That's not what they care about. They don't want to think about little Johnny flowing around on a playground in a fight with somebody. Like they just want, they want little Johnny to feel confident enough that he's not fighting at school. He's not getting picked on at school. Like that's part of it. Another part of it is that, there's a certain broad self-esteem that comes from training in a martial art. Right there is, there's just all kinds of benefits. So focus on the benefits to the audience who you're speaking to. The instructor doesn't have to write that. The instructor probably is not the person to write that. You might need to do some research, but start with the audience, figure out what matters to the audience, write that and get the instructor to kind of look at it. And if you, if you or the instructor are getting in the weeds of Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu mastery in your emails and you're not speaking to an audience that really cares about mastery of Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu at the technique level, you've missed the mark because the most audiences don't care about that. That's way too advanced at that point. They're coming, there's, and again, it's without knowing what the audience here is I'm sort of flying blind, but that's how I would think about this. Andre, anything to add on that? Yeah, I'll add that for many years, people that have reached out to us and at last do you guys write sequences for clients? And it's always a no. And for years, I've been referring people to a dear friend of us, Suras. And so she does, she writes emails for clients. And her, she does this huge amount of research. I've seen some of it, I've got a glimpse into the level of research she does. And it's ridiculous. And she does that because then she can write the best emails she possibly can for their client. So I think that's just a more general response that there's a lot of research that should happen in order to be able to write world-class emails and certainly a world-class email sequence. Yeah, so just do a bunch of research. And that's the secret to hear from every single copywriter. If you name really legitimate copywriters, they'll tell you that it's not the writing, it's the research. We're not suggesting copywriting, it's just an example, right? But if your livelihood depends on writing well to sell and overwhelmingly what you spend your time doing is research, that's a good indicator of where time is best spent. It's really hard to write something when you don't really know what you're writing about. It tends to be obvious. We've all been exposed to that kind of writing. So, a lot of it is a winnowing down process too. Have an interview the instructor and say, what's just for the sake of argument? Assume that this instructor is trying to bring students in. 80% of the students who come in are kids, everybody else is sort of an adult. Start with the kids, ask the instructor, why do you think parents bring their kids here? What have you heard over the years? Make a list, prioritize that list, get further confirmative possible, talk to people who are there, like talk to the parents if you can go there to the training area, sit there and ask questions of the parents who are sitting there when their kids are training. Why did you bring little Johnny here? Why did you bring Jane here? Just get their information, do the work and the emails will write themselves. Okay. Next question is from Guillermo. I mean, it's just think about the context in which somebody shows up in your world and start the conversation there and don't make a false dichotomy when that limits you rather than expands. We want every decision you make to give you a richer tapestry of options to choose from, not to winnow down to a point where like, oh, okay, this relationship building series has these three ingredients on this timeline at this frequency, that's far too prescriptive.
