Probably six to eight pages when we've edited them down, or have you gone away from the pre-sell site concept? Okay, there's so, so, so much in this question. I'll take a stab at it, Andre, and then hand it off to you. So I get this. I mean, I've run a little paid traffic in my lifetime, and I get it. And I've heard this argument a billion times. I have seen evidence for it, seen evidence that contradicts it, but here's the basic argument. You're paying for traffic. You don't have time for all this relationship building stuff and all the multi-page pre-sells in the beginning, and it's just too complicated. Get them on the DIML list, and then we'll let the email do the work, right? Or get them to buy something, we'll let the email do the work. That is the siren call of paid traffic. And sure, there's some, there's certainly some evidence for that, but life's not that simple, right? And we're talking about a significant spectrum of activity here. So first of all, the very first question for paid traffic is what is the intent? Is it active intent? They've got a bleeding neck search term, and they're using Google, and they want something right away, and don't give them an eight-page pre-sell site in a multi-part 10 entry relationship building series, right? So now if it's Facebook, and they're not really looking, and they stumbled on you, and they might be a good candidate based on the combination of demographic and psychographic factors, and you've captured their interest in an ad, preferably enough content in the ad, it's not just curiosity driven, but they sort of looked at that, and thought, oh, this is interesting enough to give some attention to it, and then what they see next is a continuation of that, that the ad has framed for them, and that leads to a non-bribe situation where they can learn more. Like that, it's a lot of hurdles, but the quality of that signal is extraordinary. Now, you have to find for your business somewhere in there that it's the right balance of enough hurdles so that you're filtering out the wrong audience, and not so many hurdles that you're just being gratuitous and a pain in the ass, right? So, and that's, there is no right answer to that, but it's not a question of does this model with a pre-sell site in a relationship building series only work for organic traffic and not for paid traffic? That's demonstrably false. It absolutely can work with paid traffic. It may not work exactly the same way though. So if a significant amount of your traffic comes, it's just for the sake of argument, say it's paid traffic in the platform is Facebook. There's no canonical set of rules. It's like, oh, because it's this, we're going to do it that way. It just, the ad is framing what happens next. That's the way I think it, it captures their attention. It sets a frame, it opens, it may open and close some loops within the ad itself to bring their attention through the consumption of the ad. The ad, in my opinion, is like a bouncer, in a nightclub, right? It's the person standing at the door, you can't just walk in, right? You've got to, there's a little bit of work to do. And for the wrong people who are like, I'm not interested, this doesn't make any sense. And they leave good, it's a filtering tool. But some percentage of people, this is interesting, like, okay, this just makes sense to me. Let me find out more. The landing page that they go to, you know that they came from Facebook not because they were looking, they didn't have active intent. They had passive potential interest. So knowing that framing, the question I would ask myself is, how do I capture somebody's attention? Like, what's the length of time I have that is that balance between enough where I'm reading out to people who are just tire kicking, they're not interested at all, they're in the lines of groceries counter or grocery store just trying to pass the time, click it on stuff, versus the person who is engaged, who's reading it, it goes from at least one page to another, or maybe not, maybe you do a Jonathan Boyd style that we've talked about Jonathan's landing page, it's 1600 words all on one page, you get to the end and you opt in or not, right? Like, whatever that is, we can't escape from the fundamental reality. And this to me is central to everything that we're talking about. We can choose to change the system that we're creating and try to get different segments of the audience and try to get people in our audience to do things differently. But at the end of the day, when you look across large sums of data, most people don't buy immediately. That is a fact of life, no different than gravity. It's not our opinion, it's not something that we think might be true. It's just, it's over, the evidence is overwhelming that people of all the people who are going to buy from you, the overwhelming majority of them are not going to buy from you in the first 90 days. That's fact. So we could either try to just optimize our business for the people who are going to buy at 90 days and make a business out of that, that's one choice. Or we can optimize our business so that we get those people who are going to buy anyway in the 90 days and we get everybody else. That's what should drive our decision making. So every question that we come back to, we want to come back to this, like what is it we're optimizing for here? Are we trying to get two or three more people out of 100 to buy right now? And if we do that, at what cost? Is it the cost of the people, those people who we turn away, who might not buy later now? Like that's the trade off. So that seems like a very hypothetical answer, but it's the reality answer that every decision you make about optimization needs to be based within the frame of the reality that most people who are attracted to you into your world now are not going to buy now. And the ones who are going to buy are going to buy later. And three emails versus five emails for a relationship building series, where is the payoff that you're looking for? Are you trying to get faster to a sale for those 15 to 20% who are going to buy in the next few weeks or a month? Or are you willing to sit back a little bit and say, no, we need to make sure that this system really takes advantage of the 80 plus percent who are going to buy later. I don't think three emails is enough for that audience. I think that the stage that we set in the beginning is the frame that everything will follow in. So take the time now. You can do things somebody asked later about a super signature. You can do all kinds of things to get the people who want to buy now to buy. The people who are going to buy will figure out a way to buy. They're going to do that. Just don't do it at the expense of everybody else who's waiting for you to screw up and give them a reason not to buy. I don't know, that seems like a rant. I knew I was going to rant today. But that's the rant, right? We can't argue with gravity. The fact that most people buy later, that's gravity in our world. We can choose to ignore it, but we're still going to come crashing down to earth every single time we jump. Whether or not we like gravity. Okay, your turn. Say something smarter than I said. That felt like a rant. I want to frame that and put it on a poster. Effect of Laugh No Difference to Gravity, Sean Twain. There you go. We'll write our relationship building series for those later for sale on the TLB store later. Yeah, the only thing I'll add is, when you're looking at different versions of reality, so if you look at somebody who builds funnels, like a funnel funnel, where there's no distractions, there's a single path and ABC, something happens. When you're looking at the stats, the only stats you could see is how far people have made it into this thing, how many people have bought, how many people haven't, and where the drop-off is. That's it. That's the only context that exists in that narrow path, which is not reality. That's just someone's perception of reality or forcing something on a portion of people. But the actual reality is that all of this is non-linear. People, we are people that will come in and make a purchase, and then I'll check in the system because I'm a curious person, and they're not even on our email list. Well, they are at the point of purchase, but they didn't exist before that. And then there's other people that have opted in and received every single email we've sent, and they're all green dots, which means they opened and they clicked something. I mean, there's no, but they haven't made a purchase yet. And everyone's journey through this thing, through our world is non-linear in the sense that it's not a predictable path. We can try and force some path on people, and some people will wander down that path, but most people won't, or they just won't make it to the end of it. They'll just wander around. That's, this is what we do. And it's impossible to predict what that is, other than creating a world for people to inhabit. And by them, you know, by doing that and allowing people to interact with it in a way that makes sense to them, it doesn't make sense to us, but we just put it there so they can interact with it. And they'll then purchase when they're ready. They'll do things, there'll be moments that happen when someone decides I'm a customer, but there's no transaction that's happened. That'll happen a bit later on their timeframe. That's not for us to force upon them. So, you know, I think our world, what we've created more closely represents reality in the sense that if you go to, you know, TLB.com, there is no linear path. It's set up in such a way that people can wander around and in a way that makes sense to them. And when we are paying for traffic and we sending it to product pages, they can still wander around. We're not preventing people from wandering around. You know, we put them on a page and we've designed a page in such a way that there is a sense of progression. They can see what's in front of them, but at the end of the day, they can take that path or they can wander around and go with peace and they do. So, we just think it's better to, you know, create something that more closely represents gravity. And so, that's the end of my work. And this would be my last one on this subject. I mean, don't believe your metrics either. When I say don't, you know, bunch of people are like, should I just get rid of good line lines? Don't mean that, but don't believe the story that your metrics are telling, like that's the full story. Like, it's never, you can only see the parts so far down the line. Now, yes, if you have a highly optimized funnel and there's only one way into it through a certain type of paid traffic and then you can see everything that happens in short, believe your metrics, but also recognize everybody's coming into it with a different, in a different context and people who buy, they aren't buying for the reasons you think. I mean, I just had this experience recently where I just made a purchase from a local company near me. It's a coffee roaster near me. So, when they look at their metrics, if they look at their orders as they come in, they're going to see that I'm direct traffic. So I just typed in the URL. And then they're going, I'm sorry, they're going to see that I'm Google organic traffic. It's branded. I searched for the name of the company and they're gonna look and say, oh, he lives right up the road. He probably drove by, probably knows whatever. They're gonna craft a story and their story is completely wrong, right? The actual story is I got to weirdly interested in coffee recently. I thought Gear Patrol has a website or has a top 25 coffee roasters in the United States. I looked at the list and sort of said to myself, all right, if I'm gonna try coffee, I'm gonna kind of go through this list. The very first entry on the list happens to be a roaster, like an hour from my house. And I live in the middle of nowhere. So that's how I was exposed. I didn't buy it from them that day because they have this goofy thing on their site every day of the week. There's a message on their site that says 15% off on Mondays. And I'm sure somebody there can't figure out why their sales suck on every day of the week, except Mondays, but it doesn't say it on Mondays. It says it all the time, it's crazy. But there's all these things that influenced my purchase and how I found out about, all of them are invisible to them. So the data tells them a story that has no relationship to reality. And that's why we keep orienting back to this idea that when you understand some fundamentals of reality, then you can make decisions that you may not be able to go find in your analytics platform, exactly why that decision made sense. But when you look back on your business from an appropriate timeframe, a year, six mile, a long enough timeframe, it's all going to make sense. But because the world is just too damn complicated to reduce to a series of, this step leads to this, that the world is not linear, it is systemic. And the art of email is the foundation of the art of email is accepting that reality that we're not going to be able to figure out all of these things. So what we do instead is we build a system that works as it works within our understanding of reality. And we just have to trust that by making that one decision, everything else that follows is going to be better as a result. That's what I showed us earlier. Okay, next question from Mark. This is actually a really, this is a great question. Can you say more about how you define absolutely no selling? It looks like your relationship building email still have links to your courses page and the signature. In his current signature, he uses a Dean Jackson style super signature that does something similar. And for those of you who are not familiar, Dean Jackson's super signature is just, it's something you include in every email that sort of has this idea of like, these are the three ways I can help, I can help you, I've got XYZ free courses, I've got such and such, whatever you can work with me directly, but it's a single signature, single signature and appended to every email that has kind of the overview of the types of audience interaction that you have, like you have people who maybe, you have free content for, and then you have people who want DIY content, and maybe you have a done for you service, but you're breaking it out. That's sort of the central idea of a Dean Jackson super signature. So we say no selling, but we have links to courses in our signature, these is presumably as fine to send them to a relevant valuable article or video, which has an option of a page for interested people to click through to a product sales page. I'm trying to get a feel for how to balance not selling the relationship building series while still having paths that allow fast movers to potentially discover relevant products and buy if they want to. I'll tackle this, Andre, and handle off to you, because I have one thought on this. The reason we, this is, I think, the reason we say absolutely no selling is because left to their own devices, most people who are interested in email marketing because of conventional wisdom are going to figure out some way to sell all the time. They're just going to assume that a relationship building series is actually a relationship building selling series. People are going to fill that in because of their, they exist in this, the water that they're suspended in is sell, sell, sell, and then, so they're going to add the context to everything that we include. So it's going to be like, oh, you know, like, I've got a 10-part relationship building series and we look at it and it's two pages of relationship building and eight pages of selling. And it was like, no, no, it was, I'm building relationships because I relate better to people who buy my shit, right? Like, no, stop, right? Like that's, so I think we've taken this sort of hard line position. Of course they're going to be fast movers, you know, of course you want people who are coming in saying, I want this thing that you have. We're not saying, no, no, no, hold on, but we're also trying to save people from themselves. And we're not trying to be jerks about it, but we just, we know the world is telling you to sell all the time and sometimes in sneaky ways or the world is telling you to persuade or presuade and you name it. No, don't do that. Empathy authority first, right? It's empathy, authority, credibility, sell later. When you do that, everything else works. The fast movers, put a super signature in, let them go click through stuff. I'm a fast mover. I'm not going to read, you know, the ironing, the great irony of all of this is I'm probably not going to read your relationship building series. I'm going to go buy your stuff and I'm going to get a customer onboarding series, but I don't represent the majority of people. I represent people who tend to buy things that people who tend to buy a lot faster most of the time. Plenty of examples where that's not true. So, okay, that's, I'm really just ranting today. I don't think I'm really answering questions. I'm just ranting. Okay, so why, how do you think about the absolutely no selling perspective? Yeah, I mean, you've said it already. I mean, it's just don't be explicit and overt about selling in your RBS. That's really, that's really what we mean. There's going to be, as you said, fast movers and within implicitly within the dynamic of that RBS, whether it's a general one or a product specific one, it's going to create tension and pull people forward. And they're going to want to buy anyway. And, you know, you can give them an opportunity to buy, whether it's in the signature or whatnot, but, you know, the series itself is not a selling thing. Yeah, and if we open that door a little bit, everyone's going to drive a truck through it. That's a part of it. Like just, just think about relationship building series. The way we've described it, we even named it this way, the goal of the relationship building series is to establish that relationship. And to have, you know, when everybody in your market screams, the person who whispers stands out, right? When everybody in your market is, you know, doing a two part indoctrination series for three parts and then immediately moving on to the sale. So the jack up quality of lead. And I've done this a lot outside when I was working with clients before Andre and I teamed up. I like to put email number one. But if someone reads that and they're not interested and they don't opt in, that's fine with me. The person who doesn't resonate with that isn't going to be a customer likely. So I've just saved myself, reducing my cost per lead, giving me a false signal of quality. I mean, I've cleaned up my metrics a lot by showing somebody something that they didn't need to be curious to get to see. So and when you ask the question, we put specific numbers, what you expect leads to cost. I'm not going to go on a rant. Oh, I'm probably going to rant about cost per lead. I think cost per lead to me is the most ridiculous metric in the world. I could give up as my father used to say, I could give a rat's ass about cost per lead. Right. It's AOV, right? It's average order value. And in your business model, it's average order value. And it's CPA AOV. That's the front end business model, period. And then LTV. You know, when you're generating leads in a 15% business, that's all that matters. I don't care what a lead costs. I simply, all I care about is the cost to acquire a customer and how much I make back in that customer and their first sale and then whatever their LTV is over time. Like that's all I care about in that business model. I don't give a shit about cost per lead. Because to me, it's just, it's such, there's so many ways that cost per lead can go wrong because you can start optimizing for leads, take your eye off the ball. But AOV, customer acquisition, cost and average order value clean up anything that CPL screws up. I like that. That sounds kind of fun. All right. Andre, anything to add on this first one? No, I don't think so. Our friend Jonathan, I mean, his front end is a fairly lengthy, long form page, isn't it? Like 2,000 words. It's 1,600 words. Yeah. 1,600 words. And he's a perfect example of this. He doesn't, I mean, what did he tell us? Like his, you know, his opt-in rate's like 7%, but he doesn't care. Right? Because his customer acquisition cost, and so for Jonathan Boyd, that's the person we mentioned earlier, today's his birthday coincidentally enough. Jonathan Boyd has a guitar school, online guitar school. We interviewed him last year, I think. His success in the course of a year was mind-numbing. He's a wonderful human being too. But his business model is paid traffic to a 1,600-word landing page. After somebody opts in, they're shown a believe of ESL. At the time, it was a $27 offer. His, he doesn't optimize for CPL for cost per lead. All he's looking at is cost to acquire a customer and average order value in that model. I think he's set on the call, this was last year, that his opt-in rate's only 7%. But that's not what he's optimizing for. You can jack up opt-in. You can do a million things to increase opt-in rate. You can do a million things to reduce cost per lead. But at the end of the day, we're not in the opt-in rate business. We're not in the cost per lead business unless you're a lead generator. We're in the customer business. How much does it cost to acquire a customer? And how much does that customer spend on their first sale? And then over some period of time, it's meaningful to your business. How much does the customer spend? You know those three numbers, you understand your business in this particular business model. In our business model, we don't sell on the front end. So if you don't sell on the front end, then you have to be, then you have to have something first of all that tracks in an appropriate time frame. But you also have to be willing to make a certain bet that you're going to pay for traffic. And that traffic is going to monetize with LTV, with long-term value, not with average order value on the first sale. That's the road to getting off the reservation here. So we'll jump on to that next question from the same person. What role does an ad have as a lead-in to a relationship going to series? How do you conceive of an ad writing within the context of the R&D model? That's funny. We could do some momentum build a workshop. I think about a particularly long copy Facebook ad as the frame for everything that follows. So framing is a really powerful, powerful tool. When you set the frame, you've established the boundaries of everything that follows. So if you set a frame that the key to losing weight is diet, not exercise, you've heard the old expression, you lose ounces in the gym and pounds in the kitchen. That's a powerful frame. So if I'm trying to sell a supplement or anything to do with food going in the mouth, supplements, losing weight in general, and eventually the product is going to focus on eating, then the frame I want to set in an ad is that this idea of you lose ounces in the gym, pounds in the kitchen, that killing yourself in the gym often can be counterproductive. You go to the gym, you kill yourself, you smash it at your local crossfit box, and you're hungry as hell by the end, your fatter 60 days later. That's a powerful frame for somebody thinking about losing weight, who's trying to choose between diet and exercise, and you have a solution that's diet-based, the evidence is on your side. So that's how I think about using an ad. It's framing everything that I'm going to say that follows. I also like to just side tangent. I like to open and close loops that pull attention through the course of the ad. I generally don't like to have a big loop open that requires that they go to the landing page to figure it out, because then I get a false signal that they just want the answer. But I do like to have something implicit in the ad, an open loop in the ad that is closed by the purchase of the product eventually. That there's something only for me to look down the road at and realize that the big thing that's attractive, whether it's usually an emotion, the thing that they really want more than anything else that I'm going to hint at in the ad somehow, is resolved when they eventually become a customer later on. It doesn't have to. I'm not going to encourage them to do it tomorrow, but eventually the big payoff. I like to set that up. Any thoughts to add? I know you're a big ad guy. Yeah. I'll just spare time. You're sitting around writing Facebook ads. Yeah, I knew that. That I think the big leverage point isn't coming up with really good compelling frames and big ideas and these things that people can be drawn into. It's not necessarily the underlying system. You know, that's just there. It's what you plug into it. A soap opera series is a soap opera series, but there's a million different versions of that. You can have amazing ones or ridiculously terrible ones. And it's not the fault of the soap opera series. It's what's been put into it in the same way that any system that fails, it's not the fault of the system. It's the little things that you putting into it. So I think one massive leverage point is coming up, spending time to come up with. And I think Jonathan mentioned in an interview that we did with them. It's on our website. You know, if you just go to the doable business landing page, I think it's linked up over there. He mentions what his one was for the guitar business. I've forgotten what it is and I don't have it in front of me. But things were kind of like meh until that moment. And then suddenly we can changed. His underlying thing didn't change. He still had a 1600 word landing page. That didn't change. It's what was on the landing page that changed. And that's where the leverage comes. And it's unlikely you're going to figure that out right up the gate. It's going to be a process of iteration. And the more you get to know a market and the people flowing through this thing that you've created, these opportunities are going to present themselves, especially when you put something out there and you see that the little needle moves meaningfully. And then you can double down on that idea. That's what I'll add. I'll just mention that's process to my American friends out there. You know, something earlier, here's a perfect example earlier, talking about Brazilian jujitsu. Right. So here's the compelling argument for Brazilian jujitsu. I'm doing an ad for a local Brazilian jujitsu school and I'm focusing on adults who want to defend themselves or women who want to defend themselves. It's another popular sub market. And one of the things, this is a compelling frame is to say, and you can come up with some statistic or you can just say most or whatever. But what I hear a lot is 90% of all fights go to the ground. So therefore, you want to, if you're interested in martial art and accept the reality that most of the time you're going to end up on the ground rolling around fighting and learning martial art that's designed for that environment. Those of us who are standup fighters on the take window black belt, I like to say that it's true that 90% of fights go to the ground, but that's because people like me knock other people to the ground. But that's not a compelling frame for a Facebook ad. So one other thought on this question on Facebook ads and leads into relationship building series is not to get too far into the weeds on Facebook advertising. There's no one entry point into your world from Facebook. It's a large audience that have different angles that are going to have, that are going to appeal to different segments of that audience. And what you can exhaust, one, you can have a certain frame that exhausts a tiny part of the audience. And it feels like you've exhausted the whole audience, but there are lots of different ways to come at different frames to really maximize what's going to appeal to a large audience. So don't get caught up in a single frame like that. Like Carmen, just let me know that it's not a true statistic, the X number of fights go to the ground. Yeah, I just don't, don't trust me on anything about that. I just, I've heard that a lot over the years. For those of you in the audience who are Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu practitioners, I have that respect for just joking around. Okay. You know, it's a decision that, that, that somebody needs to make is, do they want to do the heavy lifting before somebody opt in or after somebody opt in? And I've always, I've always wanted to get the highest quality. And I'm just, you know, put quality in inverted commas. It's, it's the certain type of person to come on to, to, to an email list, thinking a certain thing. Like there's a certain frame has been set up. And that's, that person's for me has always been better. So that's what I've always done. It's, yeah, I'm sure you can get to get a one sentence squeeze page working with an, with an, with an audience behind, with an obvious behind it. And, you know, maybe then your, your open rate is 30% instead of 60%. Well, we can't really look at open rates anymore, but, you know, just hypothetically speaking, could you make that work? I'm sure you can. But for us, it's just, we prefer to, to build a world and interact with people that are, that are on the same page. And I just think you can do that better when, when that frame has been set up and, and it's, and it's purposefully pulling the right people forward and repelling the wrong people. And it's impossible to do that in a single sentence. I'm, you know, I'm just flipping to the thin squeeze page being a single sentence, but yeah. Yeah. And the way I think about this question is the question you've heard us ask over and over again from William, I don't know, Ben, Dave, whatever his name is, but we'll make the boat go faster, right? So whatever is, you know, the boat, in this example, your business faster is what? Is faster leads? Right? No. Right? Again, you're not lead business. You're in the customer business. So every decision should be evaluated by the simple question, will it make the boat go faster? And faster is, will it acquire more customers? And maybe, and I'm not even sure that's faster in this case, because you can, you can really, you can acquire a ton of customers with, you know, pairing down your offer or whatever. So, you know, will it, it's hard if this is a hard one to decipher, actually. You know, if, how would we define faster? If, if you, okay, let's think about it this way. If you believe that you're getting the minority of the market now, or the methods that you're choosing, and you want to go after the large section of the audience that you think you may have been missing so far to really grow the business, then we need to figure out what those signals are that would tell us that's happening. And what we would know immediately is those signals are not to be found in the first 90 days. Right. I mean, that's, we know that we're, we're looking for the people who don't buy the first 90 days. So anything that we're doing that drives action in the first 90 days, anything we're optimizing for that inherently drives action in the first 90 days means we're looking in the wrong place. Right. What we're, what we're looking for are actions that we take now that have a payoff beyond 90 days. So what you've described here is changing up, making a simpler squeeze page and getting people into relationship building series to opt in. How does that, what impact does that have on the post 90 day audience? My, I suspect the answer to that is none. Round, you know, like, as I like to say, you know, round it up to a whole number, the answer is zero. So what I would do to answer, the place that I would be looking into your business is look at all of your unconverted leads that you've managed to generate over the last, and yes, if they're, they're sort of a, so I'm looking two places, all of the unconverted leads that you've generated, I would put together a soap opera series to reintroduce you to them and reposition the offer, knowing that many of them were never going to buy in the first place, and you're, you're reestablishing the relationship and you're moving them towards some percentage of them will probably buy. Now, if you do that really well, so you could do relationship building series, I'm sorry, you can do a soap opera series to a hard bridge, to a story powered promotion. That's what I would do with your, all of your unconverted leads. Then I would change my lead gen so that it's not so focused on leads, not so focused on even customers, I know it's terrifying to say that, but that it's much more focused on qualitative versus quantitative signal. Like that's where I would put my energy and attention. So put a few more barriers in the beginning, don't try to look for ways to remove barriers and lower the threshold into your world, look for a few strategic places to put barriers to get people who are never going to become a customer anyway from entering your world to begin with, it's not just cloud, cloud is your metrics. Okay, last, our fourth question from this same person, what if anything you do, what if anything do you do when you're CRM to handle the people, your relationship building series naturally repels? I love this question, do you have a criteria such as contact X didn't open any of the relationship building emails that then remove someone from your list or do you do anything else? Oh, so list hygiene, you wrote about list hygiene one point, not too long ago, didn't you describe the sort of systematic list hygiene? Yeah, yeah, I mean, this is scary now though, right? I mean, what's the metric can't be open rate, right? We can't do list, you know, but the advent of iOS 15, when you don't have reliable data for open rates, we can't do list scrubbing now for based on open rates. Yeah, because there are lots of people open our emails, but it's not showing up. I guess iOS 15 makes it look like everybody opened that or not, just a straight answer for that one. I mean, I think the straight answer on this one is don't, don't use open rate to decide if somebody is engaged or not, that ship has sailed. Thanks. Yeah, exactly. You can, you can store track clicks. So, you know, that's, that's a metric you can use if you wanted to, to, to base your list hygiene on if people haven't clicked over a period of time, that can still work. You can put them into a series. Lots of opportunities to click, right? This is one of those things where things could go horribly wrong. It's like, well, I'm optimizing for clicks. Well, how many links do you put in your emails? Well, shit, I never thought of that, right? Like, make sure your whole system is designed for this reality before you start making decisions based on it. I put one link in every three weeks. I don't know why nobody's clicking on my links. I think I'm punch drunk at this point. All right. So on question number five, Sean mentioned in the relationship building series video that this is a quote, I'm always terrified to see what I actually said. The system is responding to the audience. Is anything happening beyond allowing people to self select by clicking the links to be added to various sequences based on their interest? Can you do more detailed CRM configuration that work with context based on engagement windows or opens or anything else? Do you want to, why don't you answer this one first? I mean, this is like more of a, this is a infinitely deep. We're, think about this. We tend to be, because you can make things very complicated and the more complicated and I've seen some people systems that are very complicated to the point where it's near enough impossible to make any changes. It's just so, there's so many stuff and I have a friend who, who, who is very technical. He's got a course on convert kit and his, his stuff can be so, so technical that it's, it's, it's hard enough just to wrap your head around it, let alone implement it and turn it into reality. So for us, we, we tend to have simpler systems, so which is expressed inside of the art of email. The most complex thing is the story power of promotion, you know, because there's an automation that, that controls that, but outside of that, everything is just very simple. Yes. And behavior is based on clicks. That is, that is, that is another reality because otherwise what do you base it on just on, you know, engagement as somebody interacting with an email. And part of that is them raising hands at appropriate times and the only way to really get that working is for them to, to click link. So if you ask people to click a link, because something's going to happen after that, the people that are interested will click the link. It's as simple as that really. You know, our CRM or, or, you know, the, the tools that we use, they don't have the level of sophistication that maybe some of these, these crazy ones do, but that's a good thing for us, I think. Yeah. And I've worked with clients who have, I mean, I kind of get the intent of the question. And it's an interesting question. If you have an enormous list, or if you have a large list, within that list are segments that they've done, they've responded to certain things. They've, there's some combination of factors, like maybe they own a certain product that you have, but not others. You can layer on all of these things. And that's very sophisticated. And they reached out to their audience that was within driving distance. It was sort of a last minute thing. And they sort of put together a list that way. Like there's some simple stuff like that. You know, people who owned every course this client had ever created. And they were like in a certain area. And it was a natural way to put together an audience for an in-person event. My question is about providing proof in your relationship building series, proof and quotes, which gets me to come and treat now. I have experts, expertise in designing and building systems. For example, I want to help entrepreneurs build self management systems and personal knowledge management systems to help improve their productivity and workflow. What kind of proof can you use in your business if your business is new? Oh, this is a really good question. This is a really, this is a question lots of people face. Am I correct in thinking that sharing my insights and common misconceptions around productivity in my multi page presale site and sharing my story of how I overcame my productivity problems in a relationship building series is enough proof without needing testimonials and statistics. Love to hear your thoughts on how much proof is enough when you're building a relationship with new people or discovering you for the first time and entering your world. I love this question. It's so hard. Everybody first starting out runs the same thing. And there's good news, bad news. Good news, bad news is when you do this, whatever it is that you're doing for 25 years from now, you're still going to wake up every day thinking that you don't know what you're doing in the world is about to find out. At least that's true for me. So proof is far less important. And it's a distraction over just being interesting, like tell your story and think about it this way. Your story is going to resonate with people who have the same challenge. And what you're doing is you're saying this is not how you're doing it, but this is what you're actually doing. You're saying to somebody, I know you have this challenge. That's why you're here. You bound your way into my world because you have a problem that you've probably looked for solutions elsewhere and haven't found them. That's why you're still looking. And here you are. And good news, I had the same damn problem too. And I probably look at some of the same solutions that you did. And I found those wanting. And it took me days, weeks, months, years, but I finally found the thing that works for me. And in order to understand that and to make sure that it's a good fit for you, I need to tell you a little bit about my journey and what I found along the way. Now you've said it, like, you don't need to, I don't need to see any statistics. I don't need to, you're going to detract from it now. Now you have my attention because you've shown me that you and I are very similar, except you're a lot farther down the journey trying to solve a problem that I still haven't found a solution to. So now I'm at the edge of my seat. Like, what did you find out? Like, what worked and what didn't? What roads did you get out and you realize, oh, this was a terrible mistake because I probably went down some of those roads too. It's like, oh, you tried David Allen's getting things done. You bought the label maker. You did all the thing. And at the end of the day, you're like, oh, shit, this sucks. I hate this. And then, you know, you had to start over. Oh, yeah, I did that too. Like, that's what the person's saying in your head. And they're just on that journey. And no, no statistic, no testimonial, that you don't need any of that. Tell them the story, because you're telling them their story. And you're showing them that there is hope. That's the best story in the world, which is I struggled the same. I had the same problem you did. I found a solution. I made a bunch of mistakes. So you don't have to. And let me show you what I figured out. And I think proof points actually detract from that. I mean, I would so de-emphasize proof because you're in a soft market in many ways to you. Like, what's, like, what's productive? And I think about Steven Kotler, you know, he's the flow guy, he's written a bunch of books. And they have a mid-tier program, that's like $5,000 called zero to dangerous. One of their proof points is that you're like 400 or 500% more productive when you're in flow. And then they sort of, they put it in an example, like, what would that mean if you spent, you know, all of your working time? It's so detracts from their message, right? It's just I'm like, bullshit. Right? First of all, you look at the story and you realize it's like, you know, self-reported. You can't possibly be in flow all the time. Like, it doesn't make any sense. So like, for the longest time, that proof point made me not make that leap. And eventually I did, and I'm glad I did it. Of course, it's incredible. But that quote, unquote, quote, proof point just set up a potential barrier for me because it doesn't make any sense. Like, tell me about the things that I care about. That arbitrary statistic isn't. So that's a very long-winded answer to this question, but you don't need proof. You need a good story that feels honest to the person and authentic to the person listening to it. And it feels like them, and they believe that you've found something that is going to work for them too, because it worked for you. And you've explained how, like, that's how I would approach this. I wonder if thoughts, take it, take it from there. Yeah. Demonstrating value, Trump's proof for how long. And that's, that's my view. And I've, I mean, I've demonstrated that to myself over and over again over the years. One of my earlier, one of my earlier moments that I can remember is in 2006, I did a case study as a Christmas present to the people on my email list. And I said to everybody, you know, for Christmas, I'm going to do a live case study out my open. And I'm going to pick a random product. And I want to promote it. I'm going to build the landing pages. I'm going to barp, add which traffic and send it to this thing. And I'm going to try and make this thing work and make money right in front of your eyes. And at no point did people email me and say, yeah, but are you an AdWords expert? And all things like that. No, I wasn't an AdWords expert, but they were there because they wanted to see me. Maybe they wanted to see me get an egg on my face and look like a deck or whatever it was. But I had people's attention going through their case study. And I was posting all my results on there. And people loved that. And there was no proof other than at the end of it, it was either a failure or success. And thankfully it worked out. But yeah, I didn't need proof. But then funny enough, at the end of it, I now had proof that what I was doing generated this meaningful result that I could have referred back to, which I did later on. So I created proof out of nothing, even though proof didn't exist before that. It didn't need to. It was all about demonstrating value. I think we should create a theme for stuff like that. That's the hold my beer. I want to try something theme. Right. That's when you're just like, I'm going to go do this thing and show you what I do. And I may, I may fail spectacularly. And half the people watching, like I can't wait to see what I learned from this. And half the other half, like I can't wait to see him fall on his face. Yeah, that's all right. And then the question just becomes what, what, what access do you have to understand that person's voice? And what you're looking less about what she said is you're, you're focusing more on the thinking behind what she said, because then it will make it much easier for you to write in that voice, because you understand like thinking is the product or the, the written word, the ideas are the product of thinking. And it's really easy to run out of those ideas really fast. But when you understand the thinking that generated those ideas, maybe in her writing, she explains like you can, it sort of builds a case where she talks about this, that's based on some experience she's had, or I'm just making this up, but, but it's work, you need to do the work. So to be a good, and I'm not a ghost writer, I've never, I've never written anything that I didn't sign. So I'm speaking hypothetically here, but I think to be a good ghost writer is gets into this research phase again, to really be able to take the work that you have available to you and deconstruct it. Like what stories does she tell consistently? Does she tell them consistently? So you have kind of some narrative to work with, and then you can see how the ideas relate to the experience. And they put that together in some kind of dossier so that you understand that person. And it's much like a method actor. I think it's some point you'll be able, you'll be able to think like she thinks, because you'll understand how she thought about what she wrote. And that's what you're trying to get back to is not just what, as she said, what thinking did she share that led her to what she believes to be true? Is that will inform the broader perspective? I think that's, that may be a clumsy answer to a great question. Andre, your thoughts on this one? Yeah, I'll think out loud and see where this goes, because there's a few things, perhaps. In the author business, it's fairly common practice to write under a pen name, so for various reasons. So our friends at the Stirling and Stone have certain book pen names that isn't written by a single author. So there's a pool of authors that can write in a certain voice under this pen name. The readers don't feel cheated because they're buying a book with a pen name on it, because the stories that they're getting are consistent through the lens of, you know, from that pen name person, even though that person itself doesn't exist in real love, they're okay with that. And what that makes Stirling and Stone far more scalable, because if a certain writer is busy writing, writing another series, they can swap him or her out with another person who can write in that same voice. And then it's business as usual. They can get their books out and share it with an audience that care about reading these stories. And they less concerned, who wrote it so long as it's a story by an author about a character. So using that, you can do versions I've seen being done and in fact, I've been involved in one of them. So a friend of mine has a really big fitness, like a health and fitness space. And he's the front garb, although his team is so big that any emails that he writes, he writes because he's a good copywriter. So he'll write his emails. So at no point is somebody else writing his emails for him. But he created a whole bunch of other fictional characters that represent different pockets of people in different groups of people inside of his audience. And those are written as a fictional character. So that allowed him to swap out staff members that can write on the behalf of that character. So the voice is always consistent. And again, it's a fictional character. I don't remember if they upfront with the audience that this person's name is not a real person. I don't remember this as many years ago. But I think things like that can happen. And it's probably okay to burst degrees. In fact, a really quick story. This week, I was reading an article and there's this Spanish author. It's a woman's name. And it's amazing, this thriller series. And she won this big award. And she had to come to this ceremony to receive 10,000 euros as a prize. And only in that moment was revealed that these three guys are the pen names. I mean, are the authors behind this woman's pen name. But until that point, it was completely unknown. So you've, you've shared something that you can imagine the person sits back and like, wow, I never thought about it that way. So, you know, if you, if you realize that, you know, wherever COVID, whatever else you've put on an extra 15 pounds and you think to yourself, I gotta, I gotta lose some weight and your mindset is to lose weight, you go to the gym and someone says, Hey, by the way, you know, overwhelmingly weights lost in the kitchen, not the gym, right? It's what you eat, it's not how much you're moving. I think Tim Ferriss did this in his four hour body book. I just remember some example where he talked about, you know, if it's to burn off like an Oreo, you would have to walk like the caloric equivalent of an Oreo was like walking the steps of the Empire State Building. If opens or clicks aren't good for that now, how do you guys actually prune your lists? So just to clarify what we were saying, with Apple's recent update iOS 15, tracking open rates is no longer accurate. So the next best thing that we can track are clicks. However, just because we can track clicks, it doesn't mean that's something that we can optimize for or that we can make decisions about because we have to make sure that the emails that we're sending, that we're encouraging that at some sort of rate that it gives us a clear signal. So if we say, oh, we're just going to we're going to prune our lists based on clicks, but we very rarely include links in our email, we've screwed ourselves right out of the gate. So the decision to prune based on clicks and engagement and defining engagement by clicks means that we need to structure our emails. And there are lots of opportunities to click to get a clear signal. But it's still not an entirely clear signal. Some people are willing to read the emails beginning to end. You can write a good email where the links are rabbit holes, people can go down. But this is a to me, this is a big challenge right now, because people can engage with our content and our emails, top to bottom, soak it all in, transform their lives from the value that we contribute to them. And there's not any guarantee that they will have done something to indicate that that has happened to me more. Right. So I don't I don't know, I, I'm actually, I'm curious about how, how do we actually effectively prune our lists going forward? And it's, you know, it's not like the whole world just changed was iOS 15 just was released. But you know, it's that's what's coming. So we have to be thinking about this. And I said, it's a hell of a challenge. I don't, we haven't really talked about this long, Andre. How are you thinking about this? Yeah, I mean, our process hasn't changed that much because of how we do this. So inside of ConvertKit, for example, they have a mechanism where you can, where you can rank based on, on some criteria, which they call stars, so one star, two star, three star, four star, five star, five star is opening, opening, clicking everything. So I have a segment, I have a segment created for each one of them. So at a glance, like I can look now and see, we've got 20 subscribers listed in the one star category, which is the worst category. So I can just click into that and then just interact with, with those 20 subscribers, you know, just click around and just try and make sense of, of, of why they're there. And if they're really aren't doing anything. And then what I can do is I can then launch, I can trigger, trigger our engagement series. I can trigger that at any time. So I can then go into, I can open those 20 subscribers and say, apply engagement, a re-engagement tag. Well, and then that'll put those 20 people into a re-engagement sequence and all that sequence would do it. I think ours is three or four emails long. And it's just asking them. So it's, it's giving them four opportunities. It's, yeah, I think it's four emails long. So it gives people four, four attempts to read it and raise their hand and they raise their hand by clicking, yes, I want to stay. And that click just keeps them on the list. Then they're back in. So, and, you know, Apple can't block that. That's, you know, so although the stars, some of the criteria for them to be starred as a one star, the reason why they're inside there could be, could be a tracking thing, but it's unlikely because Convict also based their tracking on people clicking links. So those, those 21 star people, they could have read all of our emails. They're just on clicking on the links for whatever reason. But then when they get, when they suddenly exposed to our four email, they do you guys want to, do you want to stay email? Well, most of them are going to click and say, yeah, yeah, I'm good. I want to stay or if they do nothing and completely ignore those four, those four attempts to re-engage, well then, then we delete them. I'm afraid. Yeah. Well, occasionally piss off somebody who has been reading every email. Like, if you, if you really want it, you're in an audience of games like, but say, instead of the email, it's like, Hey, we see you haven't been engaging with our stuff. Would you like to stay? And they're like, Hey, jackasses, I've been reading every email. Now I want to punch you in the face. Yeah. I mean, we, we're actually really careful in that first email. We, we do draw attention to the fact that, you know, tracking is not accurate and you may be reading all the emails, but the reason why you're getting this is because you haven't clicked a link in the last 90 days. And we have lots of links. So that's a legitimate reason why they're receiving that email and the next one and the next one. So all they need to do is click and then they're back on again and they removed from the re-engagement sequence. And then it's business as usual. Cool. All right. So it's, it's all the questions. It's time to wrap up. I'm going to, my wrap up is actually going to be pointing to something I mentioned earlier today that I'll have it over to you, Andre, for the final thing here. So the book I mentioned earlier, but somebody posted a link to it. It's called A Gap in the Game. This is, this is a really important concept for your journey through the art of email. So here's the basic idea from Dan Sullivan. In general, what we tend to do is we tend to evaluate our progress relative to the gap between where we are in an ideal future. Right. So we, all of you bought the art of email on you, came up with some arbitrary definition of success in your mind. I'm going to do x, y, z, and then I will have been successful. And then what happens is you do a bunch of work and because, you know, the horizon, you never make it to the horizon. It's always in the distance. You do a bunch of work and then you look out to what your vision of the ideal was and you realize there's a gap between where you are and that vision of what you hoped you would do, and then you feel bad. Right. That's the gap. The game mentality is when you look out into the future and say, this is where I hope to go. But that would, what you evaluate for decision making purposes and whether or not you were successful is the gap, or is the, it's really looking backwards versus forward. So it's where are you now relative to where you were when you started? How much have you gained? Right. Or you can think about it. What's the gap between, you know, where you are and where you are, however you want to think about it, whatever is easiest for you. So when you, to make progress with the art of email, like you're never, just to be clear, you're never going to be done. We're not. We do this all the time. This is what we do. We're never going to finish. There are always things to add. There are always, you know, sequences to create, series to create and ideas to, like, it's never going to end. And if we imagine some point in the future when we're finished and it's perfect and, you know, dollars rain from the heavens and the crowd holds us up as God like geniuses, and we never get there. And we always think, damn it, we're not there yet. And we're going to feel miserable, right? But if every single time we look wherever we are, we look back to, you know, whatever, six weeks ago, two months ago, whatever it is, and we look back and see the distance we've come and how much we've accomplished since then, that's a more accurate indicator. That's the game that we've made. So I'll leave you with that today. Hey, we have one minute we're going to tie with last week. So we're going to beat it. Don't mess it up. But evaluate, first of all, recognize that the art of email is a marathon and it is not a sprint. And that you're going to run this marathon forever, as long as you're in business and you're using email marketing, you're going to be running this race. So when you evaluate your progress, evaluate it looking backwards from where you just came from in some period of time. Last week, last month, six months ago, last quarter, whatever it is, don't try to figure out why you haven't reached that horizon that you're never going to get to. And it's just it's demotivating and will make you feel miserable. Okay, we're tied with last time. So now, Andre, take it home over the finish line, beat the record. On that bombshell.
